title: Improving Generated Release Notes While Keeping Customer-Facing Claims Accurate
summary: Generated release notes are broadly complete but show meaningful “drift” when pull-request text becomes stale after QA scope cuts. The highest customer risk comes from inaccurate promises (e.g., removed admin toggles) and from missing/late migration or compatibility caveats. Improvements should prioritize a lightweight, claim-focused review workflow that cross-checks QA deltas and forces explicit human approval for sensitive categories, rather than asking engineers to maintain a parallel changelog.

claims.text: Current generation covers most merged work (83%), but a significant share of entries (14%) describe behavior that changed after final QA, with the highest-risk errors stemming from stale PR descriptions after scope cuts.

Stale PR text is unreliable after scope cuts; QA deltas include renamed features, a removed admin toggle, and a migration warning added late, and QA requests generated notes be compared against the delta log before publication.
Customers prefer concise notes, but accuracy of remaining claims is critical; an escalation occurred when notes promised an admin toggle that was removed before release.
Migration warnings and compatibility caveats should be treated as higher-risk items than routine polish because customers plan rollouts around them.

Engineering will tag final-release deltas during QA, but will not maintain a second changelog; they prefer a lightweight review queue that highlights changed/removed/sensitive claims rather than requiring full rewrites.

openQuestions: What exact tagging schema will engineers use in QA to mark “changed/removed/renamed/late-added” items so the review queue can reliably surface drift?
What is the definition of “sensitive claims” in the generator (security, data retention, access control) and how will those be automatically detected for mandatory human approval?
What is the publication gate: who approves, and what constitutes an auditable approval record for sensitive categories?
How will the system reconcile conflicts between PR descriptions, merged code intent, and the QA delta log (which source is canonical at publish time)?
recommendation:
Implement a drift-resistant, claim-centric release-note pipeline:
1) Make QA delta log the pre-publish source of truth: automatically diff generated notes against QA-tagged deltas (renames/removals/late-added warnings) and block publication until discrepancies are resolved. (S1, S5)
2) Add a lightweight review queue that highlights only high-risk segments: changed claims, removed features, migration/compatibility caveats, and any statement customers could treat as a commitment—so editors review the most drift-prone content rather than rewriting everything. (S3, S6)
3) Enforce sensitive-claim gating: automatically flag any note mentioning security, data retention, or access control and require explicit human approval with an audit trail before publishing. (S4)
4) Tighten generation inputs: de-emphasize raw PR descriptions as authoritative when scope cuts occur; prefer structured QA tags and final QA records to reduce drift from stale PR text. (S1, S5)
5) Maintain concision with accuracy: keep notes short, but elevate visibility of migration warnings and compatibility caveats in the template so they are unlikely to be omitted or minimized. (S2, S6)
